relationships.description: Basil masks the smell of tomatoes, deterring thrips and tomato hornworms, and is widely believed to improve tomato flavor.
Carrots naturally aerate the soil around tomato roots, while the taller tomatoes provide welcome afternoon shade for the carrots.
Both belong to the nightshade family and are highly susceptible to early and late blight; planting them together creates a disease bridge.
Both are heavy feeders that will aggressively compete for soil nutrients, stunting the growth of both.
Thrives in the same soil and light conditions, creating a mutually beneficial microclimate at the base of the tomato plant.
Basil requires rich, consistently moist soil, whereas sage prefers poorer, drier, well-draining conditions.
A classic pairing: onions repel the carrot rust fly, while carrots repel the onion fly.
The strong scent of rosemary helps mask the smell of the carrots from rust flies.
Mature dill can stunt the growth of carrots and, because they are closely related, they can cross-pollinate if allowed to go to seed.
Beans fix nitrogen in the soil, which feeds heavy-feeding cucumbers, while cucumbers provide ground cover that retains moisture.
The nitrogen fixed by the beans helps promote vigorous leaf growth and runner production in strawberries.
Alliums (onions, garlic) secrete antibacterial compounds that kill the beneficial nitrogen-fixing rhizobia bacteria on bean roots.
Similar to onions, garlic will stunt the growth of legumes and prevent them from fixing nitrogen.
Borage is a powerhouse for attracting bees, which greatly increases strawberry pollination rates and overall yield.
Helps repel tomato hornworms and attracts beneficial predatory wasps that manage garden pests.
The strong sulfur compounds in onions deter cabbage loopers, cabbage worms, and cabbage maggots.
Chamomile is thought to improve the flavor and growth of onions when planted nearby.
Fennel and dill will readily cross-pollinate, resulting in strangely flavored, unpredictable seeds for both plants.
Fennel releases chemical compounds from its roots that stunt the growth of many garden vegetables, particularly tomatoes.
